text: This is an interesting case of a forty-one-year-old female that was undergoing a routine laparoscopic cholecystectomy when an anterior laceration to the cystic duct partially transected it. Instead of opening up the patient and repairing this, we decided to try to do a minimally invasive repair of the patient using a laparoscopic technique where we actually cut a T-tube.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic repair on the cystic duct using a 4-0 PD suture, carefully closing the duct over a 12 French T-tube. The repair is visible in the small anatomical space of the patient.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic knot pusher to re-approximate the cystic duct, successfully bringing the duct together over the repair. After placing the first suture, an additional suture is inserted to secure the repair.

Usually these repairs are done open and many times the repairs are actually not caught until several days later when the patient has become very, very sick developed some signs of bioperitonitis. Luckily, the surgeon operating on this caught the repair early and were able to repair it minimally invasively.
The surgeon demonstrates the small size of the patient's common duct, showing the placement of a T-tube and the use of two sutures to approximate the edges of the duct.

The surgeon performs a cholangiogram through the T-tube at the end of the procedure, visually confirming good bile flow into the duodenum. The T-tube is planned for removal in four weeks post-repair to allow the common duct to scar down.
